elements.sent-2411-001: This study presents an approach to integrate innovative forms of recitation tasks into first-year introductory mechanics, with a primary focus on smartphone-based experimental tasks and additional programming tasks for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-002: Smartphones enable inexpensive physics experiments with digitized first-hand data collection outside lab settings.
elements.sent-2411-003: Such student experiments can enhance homework assignments, breaking down barriers between lectures, recitation groups, and labs, and thereby linking theoretical and experimental aspects of undergraduate physics education.
elements.sent-2411-004: To explore this potential, we implemented and evaluated a sample set of nine smartphone-based experimental tasks and three programming tasks as weekly exercises in a first-year physics course at RWTH Aachen University.
elements.sent-2411-005: Through twelve short surveys involving up to 188 participants, we investigated students' perceptions of learning with the new tasks, focusing on factors such as goal clarity, difficulty, or feasibility at home.
elements.sent-2411-006: In two additional surveys with 108 and 78 participants, students compared the new experimental and programming tasks to each other and to standard recitation tasks based on affective variables.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our findings indicate that the smartphone-based experimental tasks were generally well-suited to the students, which tended to outperform the programming tasks in terms of perceptions of learning with the tasks and affective responses.
elements.sent-2411-008: Overall, students responded positively to the new experimental tasks, with perceptions comparable to, or only partly below those of established standard recitation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-009: Given that most of the experimental tasks were newly implemented "on-the-fly" within a running course, while the standard recitation tasks have been refined over years, these results are encouraging.
elements.sent-2411-010: They suggest that smartphone-based experimental tasks can be successfully integrated into teaching, offering valuable insights for instructors interested in similar innovations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Increasing digitalization within society has significantly shaped the teaching and learning of physics \cite[cf.
elements.sent-2411-012: e.g.,][]{Chen.2012,Girwidz.2019 , especially in the area of demonstration and student experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Over the past 10 to 15 years, smartphones have been recognized as valuable digital tools for physics experiments.
elements.sent-2411-014: Their great potential is based on the high prevalence and availability of smartphones\footnote{cf.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) trend also in higher education \cite{Sundgren.2017 as well as the quality and variety of built-in sensors.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Described as "a lab in the pocket" \cite{Stampfer.2020 and "digital Swiss pocket knives for physics education" \cite{Kuhn.2022b , smartphones enable numerous qualitative and quantitative physics experiments, especially in acoustics and mechanics, at minimal cost.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: They facilitate digitized data collection similar to real labs with sufficient precision and accuracy \cite{Organtini.2022 and can measure various quantities simultaneously using different sensors \cite{Sukariasih.2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: By replacing traditional, maybe confusing lab equipment with familiar everyday tools, smartphones may help students focus more on the experiments' content \cite{Kuhn.2022b .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Their portability allows flexible data collection outside the classroom or lab, both at students' homes and in the field \cite{Vieyra.2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: This is expected to lead to more authentic data collection and data sets in relevant everyday settings, following approaches like situated learning and context-based science education \cite{Vieyra.2015,Kuhn.2022b .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Smartphone-based experiments are assumed to engage students more deeply \cite{Vieyra.2015,Stampfer.2020 , particularly in building setups \cite{Organtini.2022 , and may offer autonomy in physics experimentation \cite{Kuhn.2022b and making own decisions, which is important for open active learning \cite{Organtini.2022 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Applications like Physics Toolbox, Science Journal, or phyphox make the use of built-in sensors easily accessible and include features like data export, remote access, high adaptability to the user's expertise, e.g. in data configuration and analysis \cite{Vieyra.2015,Staacks.2018 , or connectivity to external sensors \cite{Dorsel.2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Additionally, they provide instant data visualization in various ways, which may enhance students' representational competence \cite{Kuhn.2022b , graphical modeling skills \cite{Vieyra.2015 , and their ability to reflect on measurements and their alignment with models \cite{Organtini.2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Due to the advantages and expectations associated with smartphones in physics education, a wide range of experiments has been developed and published in resources like the iPhysicsLabs column in The Physics Teacher \cite{Kuhn.2012 and specific collections \cite{Kuhn.2022,Monteiro.2022d,Organtini.2021,OBrien.2021,Sukariasih.2019,Wilhelm.2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: While ample ideas for smartphone experiments exist -- especially in mechanics, making them suitable also for undergraduate physics -- surprisingly little research has investigated their effectiveness for learning physics \cite{Kuhn.2022b , particularly in higher education.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Addressing this discrepancy, we present an approach integrating smartphone-based experimental tasks as weekly exercises in an introductory mechanics course.
elements.sent-2411-027: We assess students' perceptions of learning with these tasks and related affective responses and compare them to newly implemented programming tasks as well as for the standard recitation tasks that have been used for several years.
elements.sent-2411-028: This evaluation provides insights into the design, implementation, and evaluation of new recitation tasks in introductory mechanics with a focus on smartphone-based experimental tasks, and, in addition, programming tasks.
elements.sent-2411-029: Ultimately, this could help to create a more engaging, student-centered study entry phase, potentially addressing the high dropout and low graduation rates in physics programs \cite[cf.][]{Heublein.2022b,Troendle.2004 .}
elements.section-2411-002: State of research and research questions
elements.subsection-2411-001: Smartphone experiments in higher physics education
elements.sent-2411-030: A survey among German, Finnish, and Croatian lab instructors has shown that smartphones (and tablets) are common digital tools for physics experiments in university physics labs, with 35 out of 61 instructors mentioning their use, especially during and after the COVID-19 pandemic \cite{Lahme.2023c .}
elements.sent-2411-031: During the pandemic, smartphones enabled a swift transition of lab courses to remote learning \cite{Klein.2021b,Borish.2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Building on these experiences, Ref.~\cite{OBrien.2021 advocates for the continued incorporation of smartphones into e-teaching physics undergraduates, particularly in labs, due to their built-in sensors and analysis tools.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The author lists 75 manuscripts published between 2012 and 2020 across five journals, showcasing smartphone experiments suitable for undergraduate physics education, either in on-campus labs or as at-home experiments.
elements.sent-2411-034: Similar examples of smartphone experiments suitable for higher physics education can be found also in Refs.~\cite{Kaps.2020b,Lahme.2022,Bernardini.2024,CastroPalacio.2014,Anni.2021,Ballester.2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Their integration into university education has already been done as preparatory or live experiments during lectures \cite{Hutz.2017,Staacks.2022 , as part of recitation exercise sheets \cite{Kaps.2021,Kaps.2022,Hutz.2017,Hutz.2019 , as undergraduate research projects \cite{Barro.2023,Lahme.2024 , and in physics labs \cite{Organtini.2022 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: While many ideas for smartphone experiments have been published, their use in higher physics education has rarely been evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-037: Among the 75 listed manuscripts in Ref.~\cite{OBrien.2021 , only 8 manuscripts (11\%) report any form of evaluation involving learners.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Reported evaluation findings include successful implementation of an experimental task \cite{Salinas.2018 , an unproblematic workflow \cite{Pörn.2016 , or the perception of smartphone application as a convenient alternative to standard lab equipment offering immediate visualization of experimental data \cite{Hawley.2018 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: They noted increased student interest, motivation, and enthusiasm \cite{Hawley.2018,Rayner.2017,Testoni.2016,Shakur.2016 , a better understanding of physics content \cite{Rayner.2017,Arribas.2015,Testoni.2016 , or more reflective quantification of constants \cite{Azhikannickal.2019 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: There were also impressions that students worked more exploratory \cite{Pörn.2016 , were proud of their self-generated data \cite{Shakur.2016 , and became more comfortable with experimental designs \cite{Arribas.2015 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: While the authors were typically not expected to present evaluation data in their manuscripts focusing on their experimental task idea, the rare and then rather superficial evaluation, partly just based on anecdotal evidence, highlights the need for more systematic evaluation of smartphone experiments in university physics education.
elements.sent-2411-042: As far as we know, more in-depth evaluation research on the use of smartphone sensors for physics experiments in this context has only been carried out by Refs.~\cite{Organtini.2022,Bernardini.2024,Kaps.2021,Kaps.2022 , briefly summarized below.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Reference~\cite{Organtini.2022 integrated five experiments using smartphones or Arduinos, in addition to one to two experiments using dedicated devices, into a physics lab for Italian physics undergraduates during the COVID-19 pandemic.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The evaluation used end-of-course questionnaires and the E-CLASS instrument \cite{Zwickl.2014 assessing students' beliefs about their own and experts' abilities in experimental physics within a lab course context.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The authors report that their course was not evaluated very positively.
elements.sent-2411-046: A significant gain in the E-CLASS score occurred only in the first of the two years studied; there were no significant differences between subgroups of students who used smartphones and Arduinos more frequently compared to those who used more standard lab equipment.
elements.sent-2411-047: The authors discuss these outcomes by pointing to some poor decisions in course design and referencing Ref.~\cite{Deslauriers.2019 , which showed "that self-reported perception of learning of students is, on average, lower than their actual learning" (p.~7).}
elements.sent-2411-048: They note that their implementation of Arduinos and smartphones at least did not harm students, while it benefited high-performing students.
elements.sent-2411-049: Although the study contributes valuable information, its interpretability would benefit from the inclusion of key descriptive statistics, such as sample sizes, as well as a more detailed breakdown of groups and settings under comparison.
elements.sent-2411-050: Reference~\cite{Bernardini.2024 developed, piloted, and partly refined two smartphone experiments for a first-year physics course for engineering, architecture technology, or computer science students at an American and Italian university.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In the first task, students investigated the period duration of a pendulum using the proximity sensor placed below the pendulum as a stopwatch.
elements.sent-2411-052: In the second task, a bouncing ball was studied by deriving data from the preset phyphox-configuration acoustic stopwatch.
elements.sent-2411-053: The tasks were supplemented with supportive materials like video tutorials, guiding questions, and Excel workbooks.
elements.sent-2411-054: The evaluation included short student questionnaires, instructors' observations, and rubrics on students' learning outcomes, although not all outcomes were reported in the manuscript.
elements.sent-2411-055: Regarding the pendulum task, students liked the new and easy mode of data collection and the application of theory to real data.
elements.sent-2411-056: They disliked the lengthy data collection and analysis and reported difficulties with the spreadsheet and sensor functionality, requesting more detailed explanations.
elements.sent-2411-057: The authors concluded that "smartphone-based laboratory experiences can be successfully used in introductory physics experiences, even in university settings" but that "it is critical to reflect on the support to be provided to the students and on the instructional actions to be put in place in order to achieve the desired learning outcomes" (p.~9).
elements.sent-2411-058: Reference~\cite{Kaps.2021 reports an initial evaluation of smartphone-based experimental tasks for undergraduate German physics teacher training students.}
elements.sent-2411-059: According to the students, these tasks were engaging and fostered interest in learning more about the physics content, though they were also time-consuming.
elements.sent-2411-060: Compared to a previous cohort that used only paper-pencil versions of the tasks, the performance of the cohort using smartphones was lower, which the authors attribute to increased task complexity.
elements.sent-2411-061: However, pilots showed a medium-sized effect on students' understanding and application of physics concepts within smartphone-based tasks.
elements.sent-2411-062: In a follow-up study \cite{Kaps.2022 , a quasi-experimental field study was conducted with two groups of first-semester students: an investigation group of 40~first-semester teacher training students who conducted smartphone experiments to evaluate their physics models, and a control group of 45~physics and meteorology students who worked on analog but algebra- and calculus-based tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-063: In a pre-post-design, neither initial group differences nor changes over time were found in both groups regarding students' motivation and curiosity.
elements.sent-2411-064: The authors suggested that this may be explained by the diminished novelty effect of smartphones until the post-test and the pressure associated with the tasks being part of exam prerequisites.
elements.sent-2411-065: However, it should be noted that the circumstances of the intervention and control groups are rather difficult to compare, which likely influenced the study's results.
elements.sent-2411-066: Beyond these studies from higher education, investigations with pupils show little differences between groups working with smartphones and those with comparable tasks without smartphones.
elements.sent-2411-067: Ref.~\cite{Mazzella.2016 found no difference in conceptual understanding of acceleration and dealing with vector representations; the smartphone group was only better in designing an experiment to measure acceleration and describing acceleration in a free fall motion.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Similarly, Ref.~\cite{Hochberg.2018 found no differences in learning achievement but positive effects on pupils' interest and topic-specific curiosity in the smartphone group.}
elements.sent-2411-069: All in all, the state of research shows that, despite the broad development of smartphone experiments, there has still been rather low interest in the implementation and research-based evaluation of smartphone experiments in university physics education.
elements.sent-2411-070: Existing studies often use not established or invalidated evaluation instruments, lack sophisticated analysis, or show limited comparability of treatment and control groups.
elements.sent-2411-071: To address the desiderata about evaluating smartphone-based experimental tasks in university physics education and to explore their overall potential, we designed, implemented, and evaluated a sample set of these tasks in a first-year mechanics course.
elements.sent-2411-072: The study was carried out “on-the-fly” and exemplifies how to implement a "rapid prototype" evaluation of such a new teaching approach into a running course.
elements.sent-2411-073: To better analyze students' perceptions of these new experimental tasks, we compare them in our study to two other task types.
elements.sent-2411-074: On the one hand, these are standard recitation tasks used throughout the entire semester, which are established textbook tasks commonly assigned as weekly exercises in introductory physics courses.
elements.sent-2411-075: On the other hand, these are three additional Python-based programming tasks, which were introduced in the same mechanics course for the first time alongside the smartphone experiments.
elements.sent-2411-076: The programming tasks align with the increasingly important learning goal of incorporating computing and programming into standard physics courses \cite[cf.][]{Atherton.2023,Chabay.2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Both experimental and programming tasks were evaluated similarly, as two of the experimental tasks also required Python for data analysis, creating a link between the two task formats beyond the concurrent implementation as two new task types.
elements.sent-2411-078: This parallel evaluation and comparison of both new task types allow for a deeper assessment of the smartphone-based experimental tasks while also providing initial insights into programming tasks as another important and innovative format of recitation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-079: Overall, our study addresses three research questions aimed at evaluating the smartphone-based experimental tasks in comparison with the established standard recitation tasks and the newly introduced programming tasks.
elements.sent-2411-080: RQ1: How do students perceive learning with the smartphone-based experimental tasks in comparison to the programming tasks? RQ2: What are students' affective responses towards the experimental tasks in comparison to the programming and standard recitation tasks? RQ3: How do potential predictors (e.g., gender, study program) influence perception of learning with the tasks and affective responses?.

elements.sent-2411-081: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:design , nine smartphone-based experimental tasks (Exp) and three programming tasks (Pro) were integrated into weekly exercise sheets of the introductory physics course Experimental Physics I at RWTH Aachen University during the winter semester~23/24.}
elements.sent-2411-082: This introductory mechanics course primarily serves over 300 first-year physics majors and future high school teachers.
elements.sent-2411-083: The course consists of two weekly lectures and weekly recitation groups.
elements.sent-2411-084: Students typically have a physics lab course not before the end of their second semester.
elements.sent-2411-085: Of the 13 weekly exercise sheets, with about 5 tasks each, 12 included one Exp- or Pro-task.
elements.sent-2411-087: Table~\ref{tab:overviewtasks in the Appendix), seven tasks (four Exp- and all three Pro-tasks) were implemented into teaching for the first time.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Two Exp-task have been used already in a prior cohort but have been significantly modified, and the other three Exp-tasks have already been used in teaching in the same way before.
elements.sent-2411-089: The remaining tasks were standard recitation tasks (Rec; cf.
elements.sent-2411-090: Table~\ref{Tab:TaskTypes for a comparison of the three task types).}
elements.sent-2411-091: Students worked self-directed on all tasks in groups of three at home, except for the first Exp-task, which was done guided during the first recitation session.
elements.sent-2411-092: Upon completion, students submitted their work to a tutor for correction and grading.
elements.sent-2411-093: Each exercise sheet was assigned 20 points; in total were 260~points possible, including 50~points (19\%) from Exp-tasks and 15~points (6\%) from Pro-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-094: To qualify for the final written exam, students needed to earn at least half of the possible points.

elements.sent-2411-095: The experimental tasks (Exp) use smartphones equipped with the phyphox application and various built-in sensors, as well as external sensor boxes lent to the students.
elements.sent-2411-096: For most tasks, students received a preset configuration of phyphox, enabling task-specific data collection and visualization, thereby simplifying the application's use.
elements.sent-2411-097: Additional materials were either common household items like paper or aluminum foil, which students supplied themselves, or specific materials such as strings or golf balls, which were lent to them.
elements.sent-2411-098: Each task included detailed, partially illustrated step-by-step instructions for using the application, setting up the experiment, and typically also for data collection, covering the procedure, measurement plan, and key steps of data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-099: Common data collection errors and safety concerns were explicitly addressed to help students avoid gathering incorrect data, injuring themselves, or damaging equipment.
elements.sent-2411-100: The complexity of data analysis varied across tasks, from only reporting derived measures automatically calculated by the application to larger data processing and visualization using external tools like Excel or Python.
elements.sent-2411-101: The final two tasks, Exp~8 and Exp~9, involved more extensive data analysis, processing, and visualization in Python, with self-written code in Exp~8 and a given Jupyter notebook in Exp~9.
elements.sent-2411-102: To verify their work, students had to submit a photo of their experimental setup along with the results of their data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-103: Three Exp-tasks are outlined in more detail in the following.
elements.sent-2411-104: In Exp~2 (Sensor statistics of the smartphone), students analyze the measurement uncertainty of their smartphones' acceleration sensor in the $z$-direction.
elements.sent-2411-105: Using the preset phyphox-configuration Accelerometer Statistics (cf.
elements.sent-2411-106: Fig.~\ref{fig:Exp2 ), sensor data is collected over a set time interval.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Students determine and compare the mean, standard deviation, and uncertainty of the mean for the noise data across three different measurement periods and two different tilt angles of the smartphone.
elements.sent-2411-108: Additionally, students evaluate the provided statistics from two other smartphones.
elements.sent-2411-109: Exp~2 is the only task that requires no equipment beyond the smartphone and directly addresses measurement uncertainties; none of the other tasks force a quantitative assessment of uncertainties.

elements.sent-2411-111: Appendix for task instructions), students study the behavior of bouncing golf and table tennis balls on different layers of damping paper to determine the initial drop height and analyze the percentage of conserved energy after each bounce.
elements.sent-2411-113: Fig.~\ref{fig:Exp5 ), students acoustically measure the intervals between the ball's bounces, with the application automatically calculating the maximum height reached between consecutive bounces.}
elements.sent-2411-114: They conduct the experiments, derive equations to model the process, compare experimental findings with their predictions, and plot the fraction of energy conserved during the second bounce for both balls as a function of the number of damping paper layers used.
elements.sent-2411-115: This task exemplifies the integration of experimental and theoretical subtasks, as in other tasks like Exp~2 and Exp~4, and demonstrates how students can conduct smartphone experiments just with lent everyday objects.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Exp5_v2.jpg} \caption{Snippet of task Exp~5: Screenshots from phyphox when measuring time intervals of a bouncing ball based on acoustic signals. By this, students analyze, among others, the percentage of conserved energy per bounce for different damping grounds.} \label{fig:Exp5}
elements.sent-2411-116: In Exp~6 (Moment of inertia on an inclined plane), students modify and determine the moment of inertia of a wooden wheel on an inclined plane measuring angular velocity over time.
elements.sent-2411-117: They were lent various equipment, including a wooden wheel, screws, washers, and an external sensor box equipped with multiple sensors, including a gyroscope that connects to the smartphone via Bluetooth (cf.. Fig.~\ref{fig:Exp6 ).}
elements.sent-2411-118: To systematically vary the moment of inertia, students attach screws and washers at five different distances from the rotational axis.
elements.sent-2411-119: By rolling the wheel down an inclined plane, they experimentally determine the moment of inertia using angular velocity data.
elements.sent-2411-120: They finally plot the moment of inertia in dependency on the distance between the attached weights and the rotational axis.

By attaching screws and washers the moment of inertia is varied, which should be determined based on angular velocity data.} \label{fig:Exp6}
elements.sent-2411-121: The three programming tasks (Pro) are structured by Jupyter notebooks that offer extensive pre-written and annotated code.
elements.sent-2411-122: This high level of guidance allows students to focus on physics concepts and applying physics formulas within the provided code, rather than getting caught up in technical programming details.
elements.sent-2411-123: While programming is not taught in the course itself, most students were required to take a concurrent programming course, to which the Pro-tasks were tailored.
elements.sent-2411-124: For students who did not take this course due to other study programs, these were optional.
elements.sent-2411-125: The Pro-tasks facilitate topics that go beyond the possibilities of smartphone experiments.
elements.sent-2411-126: For instance, in task Pro~3, students simulate planetary orbits.
elements.sent-2411-128: Fig.~\ref{fig:Pro3 ), they define the function simulateThrow that returns a list of positions and times of an object thrown at the Earth with an initial velocity $v_0$ and an initial angle $\varphi$.}
elements.sent-2411-129: As most of the code is already provided, students only need to code the formulas for the time, velocity, and position of the object after a time increment $dt$.
elements.sent-2411-130: In the second subtask, students create a similar function returning the list of positions and times for a planet, based on its initial velocity and distance to the Sun.
elements.sent-2411-131: Students reuse their code from the first subtask and write the formula for the acceleration due to the Sun at the center of the coordinate system.
elements.sent-2411-132: In the final subtask, students must define another function that calculates the semi-major axis of a planet using the position and time list.
elements.sent-2411-133: The code then outputs the orbital period $T$, the semi-major axis $a$, and the ratio $\frac{T^2 {a^3}$, enabling students to compare the simulation results with real parameters.}

elements.sent-2411-134: The standard recitation tasks (Rec) make up the majority of tasks that students complete as part of their weekly exercise sheets.
elements.sent-2411-135: They are common textbook problems used for exercise sheets in physics courses at many universities.
elements.sent-2411-136: According to the taxonomy of physics problems by Ref.~\cite{Teodorescu.2013 , they are mostly either retrieval tasks involving the execution of mental procedures or comprehension tasks symbolizing physics mental procedures.}
elements.sent-2411-137: The retrieval tasks involve "performing a procedure or task needed to solve the problem without significant error [...].
elements.sent-2411-138: The student can calculate and compute different physical quantities, derive physics results, draw free-body diagrams, graph, plot physical quantities, and solve physics-related equations.
elements.sent-2411-139: The comprehension tasks involve the construction of "an accurate symbolic image of the information or mental procedure needed to solve the physics problem.
elements.sent-2411-140: The Rec-tasks are established tasks that have been similarly used for several years and are representative of those found on the final written exam.
elements.sent-2411-141: Perceptions of learning with the tasks and affective responses were evaluated in 14~online surveys (cf.. Fig.~\ref{fig:design ).}
elements.sent-2411-142: Twelve short surveys evaluated every Exp- and Pro-task in the weekly recitation groups, mostly the week after the task was completed.
elements.sent-2411-143: After all Pro-tasks but before the Exp-tasks requiring Python for data analysis, a longer survey was used to compare both task types.
elements.sent-2411-144: A similar survey comparing Exp- and Rec-tasks was conducted the week after the lecture time.
elements.sent-2411-145: Pseudonym codes were used to link participants' responses across all surveys.
elements.sent-2411-146: Codes with potential misspellings were matched if at least three out of four code elements were identical, totaling in 313~codes.
elements.sent-2411-147: Study participation, including partial responses, varied from 188~students for Exp~1 to 41~students for Exp~9; the average participation was $M=94$ ($\mathrm{SD =48$).}

elements.sent-2411-154: The weekly surveys addressed the students' perceptions of learning with each Exp- and Pro-task (cf.. RQ1).
elements.sent-2411-155: Students gave the tasks an overall task rating and provided feedback in open-text field questions on what they liked and disliked about the task and what they would like to change.
elements.sent-2411-156: Other items addressed the perceived task quality, i.e., the clarity of learning goals and task instructions, the design of task instructions, the sufficiency of instructions on the technologies used, the relationship to the field of study, and whether requirements are given and students feel confident to complete the task at home.
elements.sent-2411-157: Two items addressed task difficulty and sufficiency of instructions and supporting materials.
elements.sent-2411-158: Students also estimated the time spent on task in an open-text field and reported how much they contributed to the discussion and task completion within their group.
elements.sent-2411-159: All items in the weekly surveys were the same for both types of tasks, with only the specification "experimental task" or "programming task".
elements.sent-2411-160: Facing question RQ3, these surveys also measured ten possible predictors (cf.. Table~\ref{Tab:Variables ).}
elements.sent-2411-161: In the two comparative surveys, students compared the Exp-tasks with the Pro-tasks (Com~1) and Rec-tasks (Com~2) regarding their affective responses (cf.. RQ2).
elements.sent-2411-162: Some variables were asked in both surveys to compare all three types of tasks, while others were only included in one survey, depending on their relevance and constraints of survey length.
elements.sent-2411-163: In both surveys, students rated items about the curiosity and interest generated by the tasks, perceived task authenticity, and the experience during the tasks.
elements.sent-2411-164: In addition, the first comparative survey asked about the use of digital technologies in the tasks, while the second one asked for the perceived educational effectiveness, perceived autonomy, and linking to the lecture.
elements.sent-2411-165: Every item was presented twice in a row for both task types to facilitate direct comparison and readability.
elements.sent-2411-166: The first comparative survey was written in the present tense and the second one in the past tense to account for the students' progress in the semester between the measurement times.

elements.sent-2411-193: If a response has multiple aspects related to different themes, it is split into substatements and assigned accordingly, but not more than once to the same theme.
elements.sent-2411-194: Relative frequencies identify the most prominent themes.

elements.sent-2411-273: Analysis of students' perceptions of learning with the tasks reveals no larger differences between Exp- and Pro-tasks or between tasks of the same task type.
elements.sent-2411-274: Overall task rating, goal clarity, and feasibility at home are generally assessed as medium to good across all tasks.
elements.sent-2411-275: The trend of increasing perceived task difficulty over the semester aligns with the actual design of increasing task complexity culminating in the last two tasks Exp~8 and Exp~9 that combine experimentation and Python-aided data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-276: Students' positive ratings for the use of technologies suggest that smartphones and Python were seen as useful and manageable digital tools for the tasks.
elements.sent-2411-277: These findings show that, although most Exp- and Pro-tasks were implemented into teaching for the first time, their design was already very suitable for the course and target group, often comparable to Rec-tasks, established and improved over years.
elements.sent-2411-278: The only task that exceeded the ability of a higher proportion of students was Pro~1, rated as too difficult by 43\% of students.
elements.sent-2411-279: This is also reflected in a lower overall task rating, higher time spent on task, and slightly lower goal clarity.
elements.sent-2411-280: Reported time spent on task varies strongly among participants.
elements.sent-2411-281: This could be due to differences in how group members divided the workload, distorted time perceptions, socially desirable responses, or differences in whether students considered only the experiment or the entire task process when reporting times.
elements.sent-2411-282: The median time ranges from 20~min to 90~min, which is mostly in the expected range or below.
elements.sent-2411-283: Open student feedback from the weekly surveys reveal a mix of positive recognition and contrasting perspectives regarding the Exp-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-284: On the one hand, students appreciated the intended positive effects of these tasks, such as the opportunities for hands-on experimentation using simple equipment, quick execution of physics experiments, fostering interest, and linking theory to practice.
elements.sent-2411-285: On the other hand, responses reveal contrasting perspectives on the tasks.
elements.sent-2411-286: For instance, some students appreciated the simplicity and quick execution of certain experiments, while others faced difficulties during their conduction.
elements.sent-2411-287: Similarly, some found the Pro-task instructions clear and comprehensible, while others criticized them as confusing or insufficient.
elements.sent-2411-288: This ambiguity reflects inherent differences in task design as well as varied perceptions and attitudes among students.
elements.sent-2411-289: It underscores the complexity of designing high-quality experimental and programming tasks that meet students' diverse needs and expectations.

elements.sent-2411-292: Despite some differences in data collection modes and rating scales, a comparison with our study is meaningful as data were collected in a comparable German first-semester physics course using similar items for curiosity, interest, reference to reality, disciplinary authenticity, and autonomy.
elements.sent-2411-293: Table~\ref{Tab:overallcomparison and effect sizes in Fig.~\ref{fig:ComparisonReference reveal five key aspects for discussion.. 1.}}
elements.sent-2411-294: Students in our study rated the Exp-tasks, in tendency, higher than the Pro-tasks for curiosity, interest, and reference to reality ($d=0.48$, $0.44$, and $1.23$, respectively).
elements.sent-2411-295: This can be partially explained by open-text responses from the weekly surveys, where students described the Pro-tasks sometimes as difficult, rather complex, and (time-)demanding, with unclear instructions.
elements.sent-2411-296: Correspondingly, many expressed a desire for clearer guidance, more explanations, and a reduction in the scope and complexity of the tasks.
elements.sent-2411-297: This suggests that some students were probably challenged, possibly overwhelmed by the Pro-tasks, which may explain their lower affective responses to the Pro-tasks, as also reflected in the lower rating of the subscale curiosity/interest (short).
elements.sent-2411-298: In particular, the too-difficult task Pro~1 likely played a significant role in this outcome.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-299: The Exp-tasks (and Pro-tasks) were perceived as lower than the Rec-tasks regarding curiosity and interest.
elements.sent-2411-300: This finding counters expectations about the use of smartphone experiments, as summarized in the introduction and state of research.
elements.sent-2411-301: Specifically, it differs from findings in Ref.~\cite{Kaps.2022 , where no significant difference in motivation and curiosity was found between students working on smartphone-based experimental tasks and those working on standard recitation tasks only.}
elements.sent-2411-302: In their earlier work \cite{Kaps.2021 , students even reported increased interest in learning more about physics due to smartphone-based tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-303: Several factors may explain the discrepancy between Exp- and Rec-tasks.. 2.1.
elements.sent-2411-304: Open-text responses reveal that some students did not understand the added value or purpose of the tasks, found the Exp-tasks uninteresting or their instructions partly unclear.
elements.sent-2411-305: This may have influenced affective responses.. 2.2.
elements.sent-2411-306: Although some students noted in open-text responses that they liked the simplicity and ease of certain tasks, this perception could suggest that these tasks were not sufficiently challenging or engaging.
elements.sent-2411-307: Notably, 24\% to 34\% of students rated three of the nine experimental tasks as too easy, indicating that these simpler tasks may not have adequately stimulated curiosity or sparked interest.. 2.3.
elements.sent-2411-308: Low degrees of openness and novelty in some tasks (e.g., Exp~5, and Exp~7), serving more the confirmation than the exploration of physics content, may have similarly influenced affective responses.
elements.sent-2411-309: This is supported by open-text feedback for Exp~3, where students highly valued the creativity and freedom for own decisions about the setup and experimental design (63\% of according statements across all tasks).. 2.4.
elements.sent-2411-310: When introducing new technologies, a possible effect of novelty on affective responses is often discussed \cite[e.g.,][]{Hochberg.2018,Kaps.2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-311: Since many students already had prior experience with smartphone experiments (cf.
elements.sent-2411-312: Table~\ref{tab:priorexperience ) and the comparative surveys were conducted after completing several Exp-tasks, an initial novelty would have probably already been diminished by the time of measurement, likewise in Ref.~\cite{Kaps.2022 .. 2.5.}}
elements.sent-2411-313: The survey Com~2 (Exp vs Rec) was conducted just days before the final exam, which focused solely on Rec-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-314: This timing may have amplified students' perceptions of aspects like authenticity, but also perceived educational effectiveness, or the tasks' linking to the lecture.. 2.6.
elements.sent-2411-315: Overall design of the learning environment may have influenced students' affective responses.
elements.sent-2411-316: For instance, the perceived relevance of the Exp- and Pro-tasks may have been reduced by their lower number compared to Rec-tasks and their exclusion from the final exam.
elements.sent-2411-317: The influence of the learning environment is also reflected in the perceived weaker linking to the lecture of the Exp-tasks compared to the Rec-tasks.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-318: Except for curiosity, the affective responses to the standard recitation tasks were similar in both studies.
elements.sent-2411-319: These similarities support comparing the Exp-tasks in our study with the video analysis tasks in Ref.~\cite{Klein.2016 showing medium to strong negative effect sizes ranging from $d=-0.62$ to $d=-1.14$ for curiosity, disciplinary authenticity, and interest.}
elements.sent-2411-320: Thus, while new task formats can be successfully integrated into introductory physics courses, the potential of the Exp-tasks for affective responses has not been fully exploited in this study.
elements.sent-2411-321: The underlying reasons may be similar to those discussed for the differences between Exp- and Rec-tasks.. 4.
elements.sent-2411-322: There are no significant differences in the scale autonomy between the task types (as in the variable autonomy/creativity (short)).
elements.sent-2411-323: This indicates a similar level of rather strong guidance, typical for shorter weekly exercises.. 5.
elements.sent-2411-324: The disciplinary authenticity of the Exp-tasks is, in tendency (but not significantly), and of the Pro-tasks significantly lower than for the Rec-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-325: This perception may arise from students' prior learning experiences also from school, where calculus- or algebra-based tasks dominate textbooks, exercises, and exams, where programming is rather uncommon for physics lessons, and where experiments are often demonstrated by teachers rather than conducted by students.
elements.sent-2411-326: Moreover, students might have expected to conduct experiments in a traditional lab setting rather than using smartphones at home.
elements.sent-2411-327: This is supported by open-text responses, where some students expressed dissatisfaction with the precision of smartphone experiments and a preference for better, more accurate equipment.
elements.sent-2411-328: Conversely, students perceived a high reference to reality in the Exp-tasks, which is plausible as they were conducted at home or in everyday contexts, using students' personal smartphones and low-cost materials.
elements.sent-2411-329: More generally, affective responses to smartphone-based experimental and programming tasks in introductory physics courses can also be limited by two further aspects: First, as also argued by Ref.~\cite{Organtini.2022 for the outcomes of their own smartphone-based experimental tasks, new, activating, and challenging teaching approaches that require high cognitive effort "may have a detrimental effect on students’ motivation, engagement, and ability to self-regulate their own learning" \cite[p.~19251]{Deslauriers.2019 , as it was shown for the comparison between passive and active instruction.}}
elements.sent-2411-330: Second, the first year of studying physics is often challenging for many students, leading to increased stress perceptions during almost the entire lecture and exam periods \cite{Lahme.2024c ; these circumstances may reduce students' openness to new task types and willingness to invest time and effort in tasks rather irrelevant to the final exam.}
elements.sent-2411-331: In conclusion, the discussion indicates that the smartphone-based Exp-tasks, mostly implemented for the first time, outperformed the new Pro-tasks and already achieved acceptable to good affective effects.
elements.sent-2411-332: Overall, their perception by students is positive (around 50\% to 80\% of the maximum value of the various scales), and comparable or only somewhat less positive than that for the well-established Rec-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-333: Note that the Rec-tasks have undergone repeated use and refinement over several years, suggesting that their improved perception among students may be due to the significant time and thought invested in their design.
elements.sent-2411-334: This interpretation aligns with the findings about video analysis tasks in Ref.~\cite{Klein.2016 , a special type of Exp-tasks, which also benefited from years of development and showed clear superiority compared to traditional tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-335: The analysis indicates that gender, semester, and study program have minimal influence on students' perceptions of the three task types.
elements.sent-2411-336: Only higher-semester students have a significantly worse (P1) task impression of the Pro-tasks than first-semester students, which could be due to two reasons: first, the Pro-tasks and a programming course were not mandatory for higher-semester students, whereas first-semester students had a mandatory programming course running in parallel, newly introduced to the physics study programs at the time of this study.
elements.sent-2411-337: Second, higher-semester students often retake the course, as they either did not write or failed the exam before, indicating that they may be weaker students overall.
elements.sent-2411-338: These factors combined could have contributed to higher-semester students finding the Pro-tasks more challenging than first-semester students.
elements.sent-2411-339: The fact that all correlations between the parent variables (P1) task impression, (P2) task value, and (P3) affective impact are significant (cf.
elements.sent-2411-340: Fig.~\ref{fig:Correlations ) suggests that the dependent variables in our study capture complementary and coherent aspects of task evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-341: The direction of these correlations indicates that students with a more favorable (P1) task impression also tend to assign them higher (P2) task value and more positive (P3) affective impact, regardless of the task type.
elements.sent-2411-342: The significant negative correlation between (P1) task impression of the Exp-tasks and the high school graduation grade might suggest that higher-achieving students may have more cognitive resources available to meet the task demands, resulting in a more favorable (P1) task impression.
elements.sent-2411-343: The significant negative correlation between prior experience with physics experiments and (P1) task impression of the Pro-tasks could suggest two potential explanations: first, students with less experimental experience may find the Exp-tasks more challenging, leading them to prefer the Pro-tasks; second, students with more experimental experience might generally prefer hands-on experimental work, making them find the Pro-tasks less engaging or more difficult.
elements.sent-2411-344: The absence of a correlation between prior experience with physics/smartphone experiments and (P1) task impression of the Exp-tasks may be explained by the chosen task design, which provides detailed guidance, making the tasks accessible to students regardless of their prior experimental experience.
elements.sent-2411-345: The following limitations of this study need to be acknowledged.
elements.sent-2411-346: First, there are limitations due to practical constraints.
elements.sent-2411-347: There were some inevitable delays between task completion and evaluation, so students sometimes started working on the next task before the previous one had been evaluated; this may have influenced task perceptions.
elements.sent-2411-348: Further, participation tended to decline across measuring points.
elements.sent-2411-349: This could be attributed to several factors, including survey fatigue, a typical drop-off in attendance at weekly recitation groups as the semester progresses, and the fact that students often fulfill the exam prerequisite before the semester ends, reducing their motivation to complete and evaluate all tasks.
elements.sent-2411-350: Moreover, as students worked in groups of three, not every group member may have responded to the survey individually.
elements.sent-2411-351: Additionally, not all variables could be measured for all task types, although they might have been interesting (e.g., perceived educational effectiveness or autonomy for the Pro-tasks).
elements.sent-2411-352: Finally, the Rec-tasks could be assessed only once at the end of the semester, meaning that students had to generalize across all Rec-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-353: Second, there are limitations regarding instructional approach and setting entailed by the constraints of classroom research in a given teaching framework.
elements.sent-2411-354: In particular, this study is about a specific implementation of the Exp- and Pro-tasks, most of which were used for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-355: It was also carried out in a specific learning environment which is determined by other aspects such as the lectures or tutors' teaching skills.
elements.sent-2411-356: Thus, findings may have limited generalizability for different tasks, courses, student cohorts, or universities.
elements.sent-2411-357: Furthermore, we cannot distinguish to what extent the affective responses of the Exp-tasks are related to the use of smartphone experiments or Exp-tasks as weekly exercises in general (e.g., with other low-cost or standard lab equipment).
elements.sent-2411-358: Third, this study addressed students' perceptions of learning with the tasks and affective responses, using a specific and limited set of outcome variables.
elements.sent-2411-359: However, many other variables could be of interest, such as the influence on conceptual understanding \cite{Wieman.2015 , the acquisition of experimental skills \cite{Bauer.2023b , scientific process skills \cite{Etkina.2006 like understanding measurement and handling measurement uncertainty \cite{Volkwyn.2008,Pollard.2020,Pillay.2008 , critical thinking skills \cite{Holmes.2015,Walsh.2019 , the development of beliefs about experimental physics and learning physics within lab courses \cite{Zwickl.2014,Wilcox.2016,Wilcox.2017 , or cultivating a greater physicist's habit, which combines several of these aspects \cite{Karelina.2007b .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-360: Given the broad range of possible variables, some of which align with long lists of learning objectives for physics labs \cite[cf.][]{AmericanAssociationofPhysicsTeachers.1997,Welzel.1998b,Zwickl.2013,AmericanAssociationofPhysicsTeachers.2014 , not all variables can be addressed in a single study.}
elements.sent-2411-361: Overall, this study provides as a proof of concept that smartphone-based experimental tasks can be effectively implemented into ongoing teaching as weekly exercises in an undergraduate physics course.
elements.sent-2411-362: It shows that logistical challenges, such as providing experimental equipment to over a hundred student groups and integrating these tasks into assessments and exam prerequisites, can be successfully managed.
elements.sent-2411-363: Students found the experimental tasks sufficiently adequate, which is notable since most were implemented either for the first time or with significant changes compared to previous versions.
elements.sent-2411-364: Open feedback revealed both strengths and areas for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-365: In terms of affective responses, the smartphone-based experimental tasks performed similarly to or better than the programming tasks.
elements.sent-2411-366: They surpassed standard recitation tasks regarding the experience of competence but elicited less curiosity, interest, and perceived affective effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-367: These differences could only partially be explained with student-related predictors, stressing the role of task design and implementation.
elements.sent-2411-368: The findings highlight the general potential of smartphones and similar technologies for facilitating student experiments in higher physics education.
elements.sent-2411-369: This also creates new opportunities for institutions where constraints such as limited equipment, space, or staff hinder traditional lab courses or other forms of student experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-370: Methodologically, this study exemplifies the feasibility of (i) the implementation of educational innovations such as smartphone-based experimental tasks in higher education and (ii) a "rapid-prototype" evaluation on-the-fly into a running course.
elements.sent-2411-371: Combining short weekly surveys focusing on single tasks with more summative surveys comparing new and standard tasks provides versatile and meaningful data on student perceptions and task outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-372: The online surveys proved convenient for instructors and students, requiring just 5~min for weekly surveys and 15~min for comparative ones, i.e., totaling around 90~min.
elements.sent-2411-373: High participation rates and comprehensive answers to open-text questions show that students are willing to give feedback on new teaching approaches.
elements.sent-2411-374: Notably, some findings challenge common instructor expectations, underscoring the importance of empirical evaluations in view of potential improvements.
elements.sent-2411-375: The scales analyzed in this study can assist other instructors in evaluating their own tasks and physics education researchers in supporting colleagues through external evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-376: Given the imbalance between the number of ideas for smartphone experiments and the extent of research on their use in teaching, there is a need for more, especially strictly controlled comparative studies.
elements.sent-2411-377: Though difficult to conduct, such studies could enhance our exploratory findings on affective responses elicited by these tasks and would enable examination of new variables, such as student performance, or a more reliable investigation of predictors like gender and prior knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-378: Students' open-text feedback in our study can inform the development of a closed-ended questionnaire for a more systematic evaluation of students' experiences.
elements.sent-2411-379: Interviews could provide deeper insights into students' task perceptions and their actual task processing and experimental processes.
elements.sent-2411-380: Students' data and findings may also serve as a valuable qualitative resource for further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-381: Future work should also explore the relationship between task design principles \cite[cf.][]{Lahme.2024b and students' perceptions of learning with the tasks and affective responses.}
elements.sent-2411-382: For instance, evaluating smartphone-based experimental tasks as longer, open-ended undergraduate research projects \cite[cf.][]{Lahme.2024 could provide insights beyond shorter weekly exercises examined in this study.}

elements.subsection-2411-013: Sample task instructions of Exp~5: Bouncing ball (7P)
elements.sent-2411-394: In your exercise group, you have been given a golf ball, a ping pong ball and a tape measure.
elements.sent-2411-395: Use a smartphone with the phyphox app and the experiment "(In)elastic collision" from the "Mechanics" category.
elements.sent-2411-396: When you start this (triangle in the top right-hand corner), the app can record the sound of a bouncing ball and calculate the height from which you dropped it.
elements.sent-2411-397: To do this, the app determines the time intervals between the sounds of the ball hitting the table.
elements.sent-2411-398: To do this, it may be necessary to increase the threshold under "Settings" and thus lower the sensitivity so that the app does not react to ambient noise, or vice versa to lower the threshold if the ball is not registered.
elements.sent-2411-399: Carry out the experiment with both balls and note down the time intervals measured and the heights calculated.. (1P).
elements.sent-2411-400: Devise a formula to determine the maximum height between two impacts using the time interval between them.
elements.sent-2411-402: The ball loses part of its energy with each impact (collision).
elements.sent-2411-403: Derive a formula with which you can calculate, based on two successive time intervals (corresponding to three impacts), the fraction of energy that is conserved during the second of the three impacts.
elements.sent-2411-404: From this, derive another formula that allows you to determine the original initial height $h_0$ before the first impact.
elements.sent-2411-405: Assume that the fraction of energy conserved by each impact is constant.. (2P).
elements.sent-2411-406: Verify your result by substituting the first time intervals from part a) and calculating the value for $h_0$ determined by phyphox.
elements.sent-2411-407: Using the other intervals from a), check whether the assumption that the conserved part of the energy is constant for each impact is correct.. (1P).
elements.sent-2411-408: Repeat the experiment but place a sheet of paper at the spot where the ball hits.
elements.sent-2411-409: Vary the number of sheets of paper (at least five different data sets per ball) and note how much energy the ball retains on the second impact.
elements.sent-2411-410: Plot your results as a function of the number of sheets of paper for both balls! (Make sure you drop the ball from the same height each time.) (2P).

elements.sent-2411-411: This exploratory field study presents an approach to integrate innovative forms of recitation tasks into first-year introductory mechanics, with a primary focus on smartphone-based experimental tasks and additional programming tasks for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-412: Given that most of the experimental tasks were implemented within a running course for the first time, while the standard recitation tasks have been refined over years, these results are encouraging.
elements.sent-2411-413: The study is designed as an exploratory field study focusing on the students' learning with the tasks and their affective responses and exemplifies how the evaluation of such a new teaching approach can be conducted within a running course.
elements.sent-2411-414: In order to be able to better interpret the evaluation results of these new experimental tasks and to achieve a higher informative value, we compare these tasks with two other task types that were additionally used in the same course.
elements.sent-2411-415: On the one hand, these are multiple standard recitation tasks, i.e. established textbook tasks that are frequently used as weekly exercises throughout the course.
elements.sent-2411-416: They can be considered as an important object of comparison, as they are the most established and commonly used task type in introductory physics courses.
elements.sent-2411-417: On the other hand, there are three additional Python-based programming tasks that were implemented for the first time in the same course as part of a new faculty-led teaching approach in line with the increasingly important learning goal of integrating computing and programming into standard physics courses \cite[cf.][]{Atherton.2023,Chabay.2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-418: These programming tasks are another important object of comparison for the experimental tasks because they were the third and also new task type in the investigated course and can be considered as a preparation for the last two experimental tasks, which require Python for data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-419: Thus, a parallel evaluation and comparison of experimental and programming tasks allows for a deeper assessment of the smartphone-based experimental tasks, while also providing first insights into programming tasks as another important and innovative format of digitally aided tasks in undergraduate physics education.
elements.sent-2411-420: RQ1: How do students perceive learning with the smartphone-based experimental tasks in comparison to the programming tasks? RQ2: What are students' affective responses towards the experimental tasks in comparison to the programming and standard recitation tasks? RQ3: How do potential predictors (e.g., gender, study program) influence perception of learning with the tasks and affective responses? The focus on students' perceptions of learning with the tasks and affective responses, which include relevant target variables both of which are of interest regarding the use of smartphone experiments (cf.
elements.sent-2411-421: The comparison provides deep insights into students' perceptions of smartphone-based experimental tasks in undergraduate physics compared to two other important task types.
elements.sent-2411-422: Before implementation, each smartphone experiment was thoroughly tested by at least one person from the phyphox group to ensure its experimental and technical feasibility.
elements.sent-2411-423: The experimental ideas, for which there was already a wealth of experience within the group of authors, were then translated into task instructions.
elements.sent-2411-424: The instructions were iterated within a subgroup of authors with diverse backgrounds in physics, physics education research, and undergraduate physics instruction.
elements.sent-2411-425: This ensured that the tasks were of sufficient quality for their first use in our study.

elements.sent-2411-426: As for the Exp-tasks, the instructions of the Pro-tasks were iterated within a subgroup of authors.
elements.sent-2411-427: To go beyond the perceptions of the individual tasks, i.e. to compare the perceptions of learning with the Exp- and Pro-tasks similarly to the affective responses in the comparative surveys, the mean and standard deviation over the means (for times: medians) of all nine Exp- and three Pro-tasks are determined each and a Mann-Whitney test is performed.
elements.sent-2411-428: Overall, this part of the analysis shows that the considered predictors have rather little potential to explain differences in students' perceptions of learning with the different task types and the elicited affective responses.
elements.sent-2411-429: This is remarkable in that we observed rather higher standard deviations for the outcome variables examined.
elements.sent-2411-430: There are two possible explanations for this.
elements.sent-2411-431: First, our design as an exploratory field study with multiple surveys over the semester naturally led to a rather complex data structure with a high number of variables on the one hand and a relatively low number of complete responses in important surveys on the other.
elements.sent-2411-432: Thus, it may be that some of the predictors examined explain the differences in students' views, but that we were unable to demonstrate these relationships in our study.
elements.sent-2411-433: Second, there may be other predictors (e.g., openness to technology and learning innovations in general, or beliefs about learning physics) or covariates (e.g., related to task design, implementation, or overall course design) that are important in explaining the observed high standard deviations but have not been considered.
elements.sent-2411-434: Another interesting methodological approach (which would also require better dataset quality) would be the use of cluster analysis to analyze whether there are subgroups of students who tend to prefer a particular task type and why.
elements.sent-2411-435: The existence of such subgroups would be another possible explanation for the high variation in students' perceptions and could be the basis for internally differentiated learning opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-436: Third, this study addressed students' perceptions of learning with the tasks and affective responses using a specific and limited set of outcome variables relevant to the evaluation of smartphone-based experimental tasks and suitable for comparison with standard recitation and programming tasks.
elements.sent-2411-437: Furthermore, it is necessary to consider whether all variables are suitable for comparing the three task types and whether and how they can actually be measured in the field over an entire semester, taking into account confoundings, e.g., caused by the accompanying lecture, the other task types, or the instruction in general.
elements.sent-2411-438: In particular, we consider evidence of conceptual or skill-related learning gains to be an interesting but difficult to measure outcome variable, as the methodological requirements hardly match the circumstances of real learning settings.
elements.sent-2411-439: For example, the acquisition of many competencies requires more time than a single or a few tasks, but their promotion over longer time is often difficult to implement in given course structures (e.g., with a rapid progression of learning content in the curriculum) and measured effects are difficult to attribute to instruction due to the many surrounding factors that may confound the results.
elements.sent-2411-440: Future research could address these and related challenges and show whether the smartphone-based experimental tasks can outperform standard recitation and programming with respect to other variables not considered in this study.
elements.sent-2411-441: This manuscript contributes to the current state of research on smartphone-based experimental tasks in several ways that are relevant to both physics education researchers and instructors.
elements.sent-2411-442: First, our exploratory field study shows as a proof of concept that weekly smartphone-based experimental tasks can indeed be implemented in undergraduate physics education as weekly exercises over the course of a semester, which has been systematically reported by only a few authors so far, along with an evaluation only by Refs.~\cite{Kaps.2021,Kaps.2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-443: In particular, students found our experimental tasks, most of which were implemented either for the first time or with significant changes compared to previous versions, sufficiently adequate.
elements.sent-2411-444: As logistical challenges such as providing experimental equipment to over a hundred student groups and integrating these tasks into assessments and exam prerequisites could be successfully overcome, smartphones and similar technologies do indeed facilitate student experimentation in undergraduate physics education beyond traditional laboratories.
elements.sent-2411-445: This feasibility may encourage other instructors to implement similar tasks in their own teaching, and also creates new opportunities for institutions where constraints such as limited equipment, space, or staff hinder traditional lab courses or other forms of student experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-446: Second, the comparative analysis of affective responses showed that the smartphone-based experimental tasks were perceived as similar to or better than the programming tasks.
elements.sent-2411-447: They outperformed standard recitation tasks in terms of experience of competence, but elicited less curiosity, interest, and perceived affective effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-448: These differences were only partially explained by student-related predictors, highlighting the role of task design and implementation.
elements.sent-2411-449: Third, our study contributes methodologically to research on smartphone experiments by validating instruments for evaluating smartphone-based experimental tasks and by exemplifying a design for an exploratory evaluation study that can be implemented in a running course.
elements.sent-2411-450: In particular, the combination of short weekly surveys focused on individual tasks with more summative surveys comparing new and standard tasks has been shown to be an approach that provided versatile and meaningful data on student perceptions and task outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-451: The online surveys proved to be convenient for both instructors and students, requiring only 5~min for weekly surveys and 15~min for comparative surveys, i.e., for a total of about 90~min.
elements.sent-2411-452: High participation rates and comprehensive responses to open-text questions indicate that students are willing to provide feedback on new teaching approaches.
elements.sent-2411-453: Notably, some of the findings challenge common instructor expectations particularly about the affective potential of mobile devices in physics education, underscoring the importance of empirical evaluation for potential improvement.
elements.sent-2411-454: Overall, our study provides several important new steps towards actual implementation, systematic evaluation, and physics education research on the outcomes of smartphone-based experimental tasks in undergraduate physics education.
elements.sent-2411-455: It provides a useful basis for future research to (i) bridge the gap between the large number of ideas for smartphone experiments and the comparatively small amount of research on their use in teaching, (ii) further investigate whether there are specific conditions (e.g., in task design or instructional environment) or other outcome variables (e.g., student achievement) in which smartphone-based experimental tasks might outperform traditional recitation tasks, and (iii) deepen our investigation of student predictors (e.g., gender, prior knowledge) on the perception of different task types.
elements.sent-2411-456: This can be achieved through studies with different designs and methods, including exploratory field studies like ours and more tightly controlled comparative studies.
elements.sent-2411-457: In particular, interviews and students' data and findings can also serve as valuable qualitative resources that provide deeper insights into students' task perceptions and their actual task processing and experimental processes.
elements.sent-2411-458: Furthermore, students' open-text feedback in our study can inform the development of a closed-ended questionnaire for a more systematic assessment of students' experiences.
elements.sent-2411-459: For instance, evaluation of smartphone-based experimental tasks as longer, open-ended undergraduate research projects \cite[cf.][]{Lahme.2024 may provide insights beyond the shorter weekly exercises examined in this study.}
elements.sent-2411-460: All of these efforts will contribute to a new task culture in undergraduate physics courses that enhances traditional exercise sheets -- one of the most salient stressors among first-year physics students \cite[cf.][]{Lahme.2024c -- with innovative elements such as student experimentation and use of technology, hopefully with a positive impact on students' cognition and affection.}
elements.sent-2411-461: Derive a formula to determine the maximum height between two impacts using the time interval between them.
elements.sent-2411-462: This exploratory field study investigates the integration of innovative forms of recitation tasks in a first-year introductory mechanics course, focusing on smartphone-based experimental tasks alongside programming and standard recitation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-463: Smartphones, combined with external sensor modules, serve as a gateway enabling students to conduct various low-cost and authentic physics experiments with first-hand data collection outside traditional lab settings.
elements.sent-2411-464: These tasks aim to enhance students’ agency in independent physics experimentation and enrich homework assignments by dissolving boundaries between lectures, recitation sessions, and traditional labs, and thereby linking theoretical and experimental aspects of undergraduate physics education.
elements.sent-2411-465: To explore this potential, we implemented and evaluated a sample set of nine smartphone-based experimental tasks and, for comparison, three programming tasks as weekly exercises in a first-year physics course at RWTH Aachen University.
elements.sent-2411-466: We investigated students' perceptions of learning with these new tasks through twelve short surveys involving up to 188 participants, focusing on factors such as goal clarity, difficulty, or feasibility at home.
elements.sent-2411-467: In two additional surveys with 108 and 78 participants, students assessed affective responses to the smartphone-based experimental tasks relative to the programming and standard recitation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-468: Our findings indicate that the smartphone-based experimental tasks were generally well-suited to the students and tended to outperform the programming tasks in terms of perceptions of learning with the tasks and affective responses.
elements.sent-2411-469: Overall, students responded positively to the new experimental tasks, with perceptions comparable to, or only partly below, those of long-established standard recitation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-470: These results suggest that smartphone-based experimental tasks can be successfully integrated into teaching and contribute to refining traditional recitation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-471: Students’ differentiated perceptions of the three task types investigated offer valuable insights into how students perceived technology-enhanced recitation tasks in terms of feasibility, engagement, and instructional value.
elements.sent-2411-472: This provides a meaningful basis for instructors and researchers aiming to design more effective and student-centered learning environments in undergraduate physics education.

elements.sent-2411-485: For comparison, we also analyze three newly implemented programming tasks and the long-established standard recitation tasks.

elements.sent-2411-488: To address the current lack of empirical, evaluative research on the implementation and effectiveness of smartphone-based experimental tasks in university physics education, we conducted an exploratory field study in a first-year mechanics course.
elements.sent-2411-489: We designed, implemented, and systematically evaluated a sample set of smartphone-based experimental tasks that were used as weekly exercises.
elements.sent-2411-490: Following the approach of context-based science education \cite{Kuhn.2010,Kuhn.2010b , the study focuses on students’ perceptions of learning with the tasks and their affective responses, while also exemplifying how such evaluations of new technology-enhanced teaching approaches can be meaningfully embedded into a running course.}
elements.sent-2411-491: In order to interpret the results more robustly, we compared these new experimental tasks to two other task types that were also implemented in the same course.
elements.sent-2411-492: First, we included multiple standard recitation tasks, i.e., established textbook tasks that are frequently used as weekly exercises throughout the course.
elements.sent-2411-493: These long-established tasks serve as an essential reference point for evaluating the added value and distinctiveness of newer formats in introductory physics courses.
elements.sent-2411-494: Second, we included three Python-based programming tasks that were newly introduced in the same course.
elements.sent-2411-495: These tasks were introduced as part of a new faculty-led teaching approach in alignment with the growing emphasis on integrating computational thinking and programming into standard physics curricula \cite[cf.][]{Atherton.2023,Chabay.2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-496: They also served a preparatory role for two of the experimental tasks, which required Python for data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-497: Thus, a parallel evaluation and comparison of experimental and programming tasks allows for a deeper assessment of the smartphone-based experimental tasks, while also providing first insights into programming tasks as another important and innovative format of technology-enhanced tasks in undergraduate physics education.

elements.sent-2411-518: Overall, our study makes several important contributions to the development, implementation, systematic evaluation, and physics education research on the outcomes of technology-enhanced task formats in undergraduate physics education.
elements.sent-2411-519: The comparative design of our evaluative study, going beyond a proof of concept, allows for a differentiated interpretation of students’ perceptions of smartphone-based experimental tasks in comparison to programming and standard recitation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-520: By evaluating and contrasting these three task types, we were able to assess not only the feasibility and perception of the experimental tasks but also their relative strengths and limitations in the broader instructional context.
elements.sent-2411-521: These findings provide valuable insights for instructors and curriculum developers who seek to diversify task formats in ways that support student engagement, autonomy, and learning motivation.
elements.sent-2411-522: Concretely, our results provide a useful basis for future research that (i) bridges the gap between the large number of ideas for smartphone experiments and the comparatively small amount of research on their use in teaching, (ii) further investigates whether there are specific conditions (e.g., in task design or instructional environment) or other outcome variables (e.g., student achievement) in which smartphone-based experimental tasks might outperform traditional recitation tasks, and (iii) deepens our investigation of student predictors (e.g., gender, prior knowledge) on the perception of different task types.
